paragraphs.body: The epiphany comes instantly and without warning, washing over her and consuming her every thought. Jackie’s hands tightened on the steering wheel until her knuckles turned white, and she knew she had forgotten something. A second ago the drive had been peaceful, going exactly to plan. Whatever had triggered this realization, she wasn’t sure if she was grateful for it or hated that it had taken her this long to notice. Maybe she hadn’t felt it in her jacket and wasn’t aware of it until mindlessly shifting in her seat, or she had zoned out just enough to become conscious of herself and everything she had done to prepare that day.  She was completely appalled by her own stupidity. Jackie never forgot anything. Everything was always planned down to the letter, with no loose ends or possibilities even given the chance to show their faces. When she forgot she felt sloppy, unprepared, and idiotic. On any regular drive, forgetting would not matter. Today of all days though, it certainly complicated things. She quickly darted her eyes to the girl in the passenger seat, hoping she wasn’t aware of the cyclone of thoughts going on 2 feet away from her.

annotations.text: she knew she had forgotten something
annotations.comment: I love this title. Is this the epiphany?
Love this depiction of Jackie, who seems tightly wound!
I like this beginning a lot but I couldn’t quite tell if she knew what she had forgotten or not. Is the epiphany, oh, shit I forgot _____, or oh, shit, I forgot something, what is it?

paragraphs.body: My favorite person in the world glances over at me while I’m staring out the windshield, basking in my perfect life. I am wearing my best dress, the blue floral one that Jackie says brings out my eyes. The air smells like ocean salt, distant campfires, and being in love. I’ve been suspecting that Jackie was going to propose to me for about a month, and I was betting that today was the day. Since we woke up this morning, she’s had that look in her eye that she is planning something and has been far more on edge than usual. She insisted that we wear something nice because she wanted to take pictures on the beach after we had our monthly picnic together. My friends think it’s cheesy that we keep up old traditions like this and ask if we ever get bored of eating the same sandwiches on the same beach on the same day month after month. I try to explain but none of them know what it's like to be in love with Jackie. She makes trivial things seem remarkable, especially Italian subs on the shore.

annotations.comment: The voice of this speaker felt a little childish to me (maybe because of “the girl” prior) and I was surprised to realize this is the first speaker’s girlfriend, not her daughter.
Here, I can see the speaker as a grown up much better.
annotations.context_right: e world glances over at me while I’m staring out the windshield, basking in my perfect life. I am we

annotations.comment: I’m reminded of something I once heard — there’s someone for everyone, you just have to find the person whose crazy matches yours.

paragraphs.body: The passive grin on Sloane’s face tells Jackie that her passenger doesn’t have a clue what’s happening. Good, this gives her time to think about how to right her mistake. How could she forget the most important part of today? They had the same damn picnic on the second of every month and she had managed to forget the singular difference that made today special. At the beginning of their relationship, Jackie had scoffed at the idea of celebrating every single monthly anniversary in this way, but when they had done it enough times it became something she started to look forward to. It gave her time to turn off her thoughts and just exist with Sloane. There was no room for that now because she had forgotten. Approaching landmarks ahead told Jackie that their short beachside drive was nearly over, and she had to think fast. How would she do it? Her unfortunate lapse in memory had thrown a wrench in her original plan. She had wanted to put it in the picnic basket and bring it out at the end when they had finished their sandwiches, presenting it to Sloane along with a meticulously written speech. At the risk of Sloane finding it, she could not have added it to the basket until right before they headed to their spot on the beach. This new addition was a break in the routine, something she wasn’t used to, something she hadn’t been quite sure about until the day before this picnic.

annotations.comment: I was a little confused here because it sure seemed like it was Jackie’s idea to picnic! However, I do enjoy that they each think that they’re kinda of doing it for the other.
You might consider what threw her off to forget about the ring. (Also, why are you hiding that it’s a ring, if I’m correct about that? Feels sort of obvious…)

paragraphs.body: I’ve always thought that Jackie’s best quality was her ability to forgive. I never felt afraid to make a mistake because I knew that she would be with me through it and never be judgmental. The car passed the tree we always said looked like an outstretched hand, perpendicular to the ground, fingers spread wide, signaling STOP. Our relationship had had ups and downs like any other, and my mistakes had almost brought us down once, but Jackie had fought for us to keep going. She forgave, because that’s the type of person she is.  Four months ago, I went out without Jackie. She had to work the next day but insisted I still have a good time. A day doesn’t go by that I don’t wish I had stayed home with her and gone to bed early. The club lights had been bright and disorienting, and I had been far too drunk to have a firm grasp on reality. “It wasn’t my fault.” That’s what I repeated to Jackie the morning after when she saw my bruised neck and realized what I did. “It didn’t mean anything,” I said it through tears, on my knees begging for forgiveness. It was a fight that lasted days. I had done everything I could to make Jackie love me again. Just when she was starting to come around, I’d told her something I should have taken to my grave. “She looked just like you.” I regretted it the second I’d said it. I expected Jackie to scream or pack a bag. Instead, she looked at me with completely dead eyes. She told me to never mention her again and we would be okay. I love Jackie because Jackie forgives. Because Jackie forgives, I’m as sure as ever that the rest of our lives are going to start today.

annotations.comment: This made me feel concerned. Jackie doesn’t really seem like the forgiving type, alas.
Four months is a long time and if whatever Jackie is doing now is her revenge, I wondered why today? (Don’t get me wrong, I think it’s a fun twist — not a ring after all! — but why today?)
I find this Sloane to be more believable than the “everything is perfect” Sloane of above.
Again, with the big time jump, why are they both thinking about this today? Is it an anniversary?

paragraphs.body: Seeing her girlfriend come home covered in someone else’s lipstick had been the second worst day of Jackie’s life. She turned off at the right exit and knew they were now five minutes away from the parking lot. A spray of ocean breeze came through the open window, slightly dampening her face. Nearly twenty-four hours ago, Sloane had been in the shower and Jackie had been making up the basket for their picnic like she always did. A notification lit up Sloane’s phone from a name Jackie didn’t recognize. She wasn’t proud of it, but she had taken a look and seen four months of messages. A stranger who looked just like her wanted to know when they could meet up next. A week without her was “too long.” She missed Sloane. She loved Sloane. Jackie had felt her heart fall to her stomach.

annotations.comment: I don’t mind this twist, but it doesn’t track with the prior paragraph, in which Sloane went to such lengths to say that she hadn’t done anything more. Why lie to readers?

paragraphs.body: When they turned into the lot, Sloane was smiling ear to ear. Jackie pulled into their usual parking spot, the sun at its highest point, shining into Sloane’s wide clueless eyes. Jackie hadn’t even remembered buying the gun. All she remembered was waking from her daze with it next to her in the passenger seat, where Sloane sat right now. That gun was currently sitting in the bottom of her sock drawer, where she hid everything she didn’t want Sloane to find. She felt like an amateur for forgetting it. She was an amateur, she reminded herself. This would be her first and only one. She wouldn’t get caught and she would go about her life without Sloane for the first time in three years. Without the gun, it would be a much messier, physical endeavor, but not difficult. Jackie had thought about alternate methods on her way to buy the gun. She was much stronger and taller than Sloane, so overpowering her wouldn’t be an issue. They exited the car, and Jackie flashed a fake smile. Her mind fully recovered from the misstep in her plan, she picked up the picnic basket and walked onto the beach full of purpose and determination. It was her anniversary, and she was going to kill her girlfriend.

annotations.text: This would be her first and only one

paragraphs.body: I love Jackie. She’s the only thing I’ve ever loved like this. She is perfect in every way, and I hope she gets everything she wants.

annotations.comment: I mean, I love the drama of it, but it’s a little hard to follow why they don’t just break up. I guess I buy that Jackie’s too proud but Sloane, why hasn’t she just left her for the other girl? Murder seems extreme! All of that said, Josephine, I enjoyed this draft. It’s a fun twist on the revenge killing trope and I really liked how you got me t think it was an engagement ring for a long time! I do think you’re going to have to deal with a couple of issues here. First, I really wanted to know why this day was the day Jackie chose (since they had picnicked at least 3 times before this time since she found out about the one night stand). Is it because of finding the texts? Why go to such an extreme length, too? And the plan feels — not great, in terms of getting away with it, with or without the gun. I had trouble tracking Sloane — she lies and also seems to be deluded at times which made me dislike her. Have you considered including some of her dialogue instead of her thoughts? Telling the story only from Jackie’s perspective might help you out because we’d only hear what Sloane says, and what Jackie thinks about it, rather than what you’re doing here which ends up being pretty confusing. Overall, really fun! Happy revising!
